lesson.menu_desc: Getting around, relative clauses & a reading on exploring cities -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 8 -- Navigating New Cities
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 8 -- Navigating New Cities

reading.rtitle: The City That Rewards the Lost
paras: When Mariana first arrived in Lisbon, she made the mistake that ruins so many trips: she tried to control everything. She studied the metro map for hours, bought a guidebook that promised the ten unmissable sights, and plotted a route that would let her tick every box before sunset. By noon on the first day she was exhausted, irritable, and oddly disappointed. The city, which she had imagined for years, felt like a checklist she was failing to complete.

Everything changed on the afternoon she got lost. A tram broke down on a street that she could not find on any map, so she simply started walking uphill, away from the crowds. The neighbourhood where she ended up, a maze of crooked lanes and tiled facades, was not in her guidebook at all. She found a tiny bakery whose owner spoke no English, a viewpoint that looked out over the whole river, and a square where old men played cards in the shade. None of it was planned, and that, she realised, was exactly the point.

Mariana now travels differently. She still reads about a place before she goes, but she leaves whole afternoons empty for the streets that nobody recommends. The traveller who insists on a perfect itinerary, she says, never meets the city that actually exists. A walkable town, which forgives a wrong turn and turns it into a discovery, will always teach you more than a car window ever could. Getting pleasantly lost, it turns out, is not a failure of planning. It is the plan.

gist.prompt: What is the writer's main point about Mariana's trip?
choices: A detailed itinerary and a good guidebook are the secret to enjoying a new city.
Leaving room to wander and get lost lets a traveller discover the real city.
Cities are best explored quickly by car so you can see every famous sight.

matching.title: Match the city expression to its meaning
defs: a high spot from which you can see far across a place
away from the busy, touristy areas
a planned schedule of places to visit on a trip
to wander without a fixed route and enjoy the surprise
a place where it is easy and pleasant to go around on foot

parts: Mariana got lost was a maze of crooked lanes. She found a bakery
owner spoke no English, a viewpoint
looked out over the river, and a traveller
insists on a perfect plan, she says, never meets the city

items: On her first day in Lisbon, Mariana tried to plan and control everything.
t
She studied the map, bought a guidebook, and plotted a route to tick every box.
Mariana's best discoveries that day were all listed in her guidebook.
f
The neighbourhood where she ended up was not in her guidebook at all.
She got lost because a tram broke down on a street she could not find on a map.
t
A tram broke down on a street that she could not find on any map, so she walked uphill.
Mariana now believes a strict itinerary is the only way to enjoy a city.
f
She leaves afternoons empty and says a perfect itinerary stops you meeting the real city.
Describe a place you discovered by accident, using a relative clause: "a square where...", "a person who...".
Are you a planner or a wanderer when you travel? Why?
Think of a city you know well. Would you call it walkable or car-dependent, and which detail makes it so?
Mariana says getting lost "is the plan". Do you agree? When does that work, and when does it not?

rephrase.title: Combine each pair into one sentence using a relative clause
items: Mariana found a bakery. Its owner spoke no English.
whose
She reached a viewpoint. It looked out over the whole river.
which
She loved the neighbourhood. She got lost there.
where
The traveller never meets the real city. The traveller insists on a perfect plan.
who
A walkable town forgives a wrong turn. It turns the wrong turn into a discovery.
which

answerkey.title: Reveal the combine answer key
list: Mariana found a bakery whose owner spoke no English.
She reached a viewpoint which looked out over the whole river.
She loved the neighbourhood where she got lost.
The traveller who insists on a perfect plan never meets the real city.
A walkable town, which forgives a wrong turn, turns it into a discovery.
answerkey.note: Defining clauses (sentences 1-4) carry essential information and take NO commas; you may use that for things instead of which. A non-defining clause (sentence 5) adds extra information, so it is wrapped in commas and you can NEVER use that -- only which or who.

listenings.text: Right, hop in. Now, the road that usually takes you downtown is completely closed for repairs, so we will have to take a detour. Don't worry -- I know a shortcut, which most tourists never find. We will avoid the worst of the congestion near the river, where the traffic at rush hour is honestly a nightmare. There is a roundabout coming up; the third exit, which is the one we want, leads straight to your hotel. If you ever come back, my advice is simple: never drive yourself in this city. Use public transit, or hail a cab like a local. It is faster, cheaper, and far less stressful.

I used to be terrified of getting around in a city I didn't know. These days, I actually enjoy it. The trick, which took me years to learn, is to slow down and read the place. I look for the crosswalks where locals gather, the one-way streets that only buses use, and the little shortcuts that never appear on a map. My favourite moment is rush hour, when the whole city is in motion -- the commuters who rush past, the cyclists who weave through the gridlock. A new city, which once felt like a maze, becomes a puzzle I love to solve. That, for me, is the real joy of travel.
